summary: The post challenges the common misconception that large tech companies are bloated and wasteful by explaining why products like Google Search require thousands of engineers despite seeming simple on the surface. Luu demonstrates that building an MVP in a weekend is vastly different from building a production system at scale, which requires extensive work on performance optimization, internationalization, security, error handling, and countless revenue-driving features that users don't even notice. He argues that companies rationally keep hiring engineers as long as each additional engineer generates more value through cost savings, performance improvements, or revenue-increasing features than they cost to employ.
key_points: Building a simple MVP versus a production system at scale are entirely different problems - the "real" work is in all the aspects people don't think about
Performance optimization alone justifies many engineers because even small latency improvements significantly impact revenue and reduce infrastructure costs
Internationalization is far more complex than people realize - each language has unique technical challenges that require specialized knowledge
Organizational inefficiencies are much harder than technical problems - message loss and corruption rates in human organizations far exceed those in distributed systems
Companies rationally hire until marginal cost equals marginal benefit - if an engineer can add percentage points to revenue or reduce costs, they're worth hiring
money_quotes: When people imagine how long it should take to build something, they're often imagining a team that works perfectly and spends 100% of its time coding. But that's impossible to scale up.

Distributed systems are considered hard because real systems might drop something like 0.1% of messages, corrupt an even smaller percentage of messages, and see latencies in the microsecond to millisecond range. When I talk to higher-ups and compare what they think they're saying to what my coworkers think they're saying, I find that the rate of lost messages is well over 50%, every message gets corrupted, and latency can be months or years.

For reliable systems, error handling is more work than the happy path. The same thing is true for large services -- all of this stuff that people don't think of as 'real' work is more work than the core service.
A company that could eliminate organizational inefficiency would be a larger innovation than any tech startup, ever.
It's fine to ignore this stuff for a weekend-project MVP, but ignoring it in a real business means ignoring the majority of the market!
key_insight: The vast majority of engineering work at large tech companies goes into optimization, internationalization, reliability, and incremental features that each add small revenue gains - work that's invisible to outsiders but essential for profitability at scale.
